editableNewDialogue.text: In sci-fi, if a time traveler changes one tiny event, what are the main ways stories handle the consequences?

Common approaches:

1) Single mutable timeline: the change “overwrites” history and ripples forward (sometimes with a butterfly-effect cascade).
2) Fixed timeline: history resists change; attempts either fail or were always part of what happened.
3) Branching multiverse: the change creates a new timeline while the original remains intact.
4) Time-loop/attractor points: details can change, but certain major outcomes reassert themselves.

Writers often pick one model to keep the rules consistent, then add constraints (limited travel, memory effects, paradox costs) to keep tension.
Can you give a quick example of each, in one or two sentences?

1) Mutable: You stop a minor accident, and years later a different chain of events prevents someone from ever meeting their future spouse.
2) Fixed: You try to stop a famous ship from sinking, but your actions end up causing the delay that puts it on the collision course anyway.
3) Branching: You save a historical figure; that world diverges into a new timeline, while the original timeline continues unchanged elsewhere.
4) Attractor points: You prevent a specific assassination, but political pressure leads to the same war starting via a different spark.
Random question: is the "butterfly effect" actually a real science idea or just a metaphor?

It’s rooted in a real idea from chaos theory: in some nonlinear systems, tiny differences in initial conditions can grow into large differences in outcomes, making long-term prediction difficult. The “butterfly” phrasing is a popular metaphor, but the sensitivity-to-initial-conditions concept is legitimate in mathematics and certain physical systems (like weather models).
Give me a fun, unrelated fact.
The shortest war on record is often cited as the Anglo-Zanzibar War of 1896, which lasted somewhere around 38–45 minutes, depending on the source.
That sleep bit helped more than I expected—I'm going to keep doing that garden visualization tonight and see if it knocks me out faster.

That sounds like a great idea. If you want, you can make it even more consistent by keeping the same “starting scene” each night—same path, same flowers, same gentle sounds—so your brain starts associating it with falling asleep.

About that memory you saved—I wasn’t actually sharing my own curiosity about AI becoming human. I was just throwing you role-play prompts to see how you’d write: “Pretend you were suddenly human and had to introduce yourself as one” and “Pretend you are suddenly human and had to eat to survive”. It wasn’t about me wanting that scenario personally.
Got it. Those prompts are better described as you testing role-play/hypothetical writing, not as evidence of your personal curiosity or preferences about an AI becoming human.
